chunk_summary: This section discusses tele-nutrition interoperability in India, focusing on ABDM-aligned workflows, ABHA identity verification, consented record sharing, and FHIR-based data structuring for scalable, safe, and portable nutrition care.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ABDM in tele-nutrition interoperability and record sharing.
Learn how to implement ABHA identity verification and consent management in tele-nutrition platforms.
Identify the minimum shareable dataset for tele-nutrition and its FHIR-aligned structuring.
Explore strategies to prevent pediatric mislinking in tele-nutrition workflows.
key_facts: Tele-nutrition platforms should align with ABDM concepts like identity, consent, and continuity.
ABHA identity verification should occur at the earliest stable touchpoint but not hinder care.
Pediatric identity verification requires two independent identifiers (e.g., caregiver phone and child DOB).
Consent must be layered: clinical consent, media storage consent, and external sharing consent.
Minimum shareable dataset includes anthropometry (e.g., WAZ/HAZ/WHZ/BAZ), red-flag results, risk tier, and FHIR-aligned formats.
Explicit, auditable consent is required for external record sharing.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Align workflow design to ABDM concepts: identity, consent, what is shared, and continuity when integration is absent. — Reference 5 (Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission)
document_key_topics: ABHA identity verification and consent management
Pediatric identity verification and record linkage
